coredata.dc:title: Interfacial charge changes induce the responsive behavior of two analytes based on novel carbon nanomaterials
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yaowen
Chen, Junjie
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: Nanoscale technique has been regarded as the manipulation of organic and inorganic entities at the atomic level and unique features are acquired. Such surface-passivated nanodomains are exploring diverse roles by expanding the range of their prospects. The well-defined fluorescent framework based on carbon quantum dots has been described, which can be utilized to detect Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA). The growth of carbon dots has been realized in the presence of 3-Aminophenylboronic acid and citric acid and the synthesis route is based on hydrothermal condition. Under the excitation at 365 nm, the nanoparticles give rise to intensive blue emission at 450 nm. The addition of Fe3+ induces the signal suppression. Meanwhile, the presence of ascorbic acid can substantially recover the emission, which will be caused by the reduction of trivalent iron ions by ascorbic acid. The titration profile for both Fe3+ and ascorbic acid in the broad range of 5-450 μM and 10-350 μM are in the forms of linear equations. The relative low detection limits (394 nM and 448 nM) are determined for Fe3+ and AA respectively. The development of multiple targets sensing on the basis of carbon dots achieves a deeper understanding of fundamental properties in nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Sensor
Dynamic quenching
Double-target determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe(Ⅲ) and ascorbic acid

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Q. M. thanks grants from Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2021A1515010324 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthetic route and detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid.
UV–vis spectra of CDs and CDs-Fe3+ and excitation spectrum of CDs.
Emission spectra of CDs upon addition of 0–900 μM of Fe3+ (Inset Figure: CDs solution in the absence (left side) and presence (right side) of Fe3+at 900 μM).
Emission spectra of CDs- Fe3+ upon addition of 0–450 μM of AA (Inset Figure: CDs-Fe3+ solution in the absence (left side) and presence (right side) of AA at 450 μM).
Zeta potential of CDs and CDs-Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Interfacial charge changes induce the responsive behavior of two analytes based on novel carbon nanomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoscale technique has been regarded as the manipulation of organic and inorganic entities at the atomic level and unique features are acquired. Such surface-passivated nanodomains are exploring diverse roles by expanding the range of their prospects. The well-defined fluorescent framework based on carbon quantum dots has been described, which can be utilized to detect Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA). The growth of carbon dots has been realized in the presence of 3-Aminophenylboronic acid and citric acid and the synthesis route is based on hydrothermal condition. Under the excitation at 365 nm, the nanoparticles give rise to intensive blue emission at 450 nm. The addition of Fe3+ induces the signal suppression. Meanwhile, the presence of ascorbic acid can substantially recover the emission, which will be caused by the reduction of trivalent iron ions by ascorbic acid. The titration profile for both Fe3+ and ascorbic acid in the broad range of 5-450 μM and 10-350 μM are in the forms of linear equations. The relative low detection limits (394 nM and 448 nM) are determined for Fe3+ and AA respectively. The development of multiple targets sensing on the basis of carbon dots achieves a deeper understanding of fundamental properties in nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Particle size, surface charge and high aqueous solubility make carbon quantum dots eligible for the specific detection of guest species under diverse conditions especially in complex and competitive medium. The exploration of new analytes for quantitative determination will be the main task for such new nanoplatforms.

As a sort of metal ions, Fe3+ is widely distributed in the earth and it can be considered as the most important trace elements in biological activities [1–6]. A few publications reported Fe3+ exhibits different functions in a series of biochemical reactions including catalytic enzyme reaction [7,8], charge migration [9,10], and hemoglobin carrying oxygen [11,12]. The slight change for this element will cause unexpected results related to health status. When its level is below the safety standard, a variety of nutritional problems such as anemia and mental retardation will become serious. Simultaneously, the health of human body is highly relevant to the level of ascorbic acid, the lower concentration of ascorbic acid would trigger neurological or tumor-related disease. It also could contribute to the formation of collagen. As a result, the accurate detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid is an indispensable factor in the field of physiological range. In current status, most of developed probes are focused on the determination of single analyte. As for the requirement of practical uses, the responses to more than one target would be more significant and such active materials would make the recognition process much more effective.

Quite a few cost-effective ways such as photoluminescence studies have been selected to detect metal ions and there will be no complex instruments or procedures. Numerous organic fluorescent dyes or metal complexes were designed and prepared to exhibit the potential for analytes sensing [13–16]. The stability was found to much lower than expectation. Thus a few semiconductor quantum dots were developed. However, the encapsulation of heavy metal in CdS or CdSe would possibly lead to leaking problems and more environmental-friendly active materials would be required.

In this way, carbon quantum dots (CDs) offer much better performance in optical sensing due to their excellent water solubility [17], convenient preparation [18], light stability [19], limited toxicity [20] and adjustable surface groups [21–27]. To demonstrate the reliability of metal detection potentials, the corresponding studies concerning synthesis and photophysical property have been evaluated.

Approaches for the assembly of carbon dots can be variable and many methods including combustion, ultrasonic synthesis and solar illumination have been described. To our concern, the simple way to achieve homogenous particles and stable properties, the better. Accordingly, the traditional bottom-up method-hydrothermal treatment has been chosen to afford the regular microstructures. Following reaction in a Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave for 8 hours, the blue carbon dots were synthesized via 3-aminophenylboronic acid and citric acid as raw materials. Such nano-platform has been employed to monitor the concentration of Fe3+, and an “on- off” effect closely related to Fe3+ was identified and the incorporation of ascorbic acid could re-generate its emission again.

3-aminophenylboronic acid (98 %) was purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, P. R. China). Citric acid (99.5%) was provided by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Deionized water was used throughout this investigation. All the other regular reagents used were of analytic pure grade.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were monitored by a Hitachi FL4600 spectrophotometer. Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra were collected by a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrophotometer. The microstructures were analyzed by a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL JEM-2100F). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured within the 4000-400 cm−1 region on an infrared spectrophotometer (Prestingge- 21). A KBr pellet technique was employed for the sample preparation. The quantitative data for absolute quantum yields was measured at ambient condition via a Hamamatsu Photonics measurement system (λex = 365 nm).

Two carbon precursors were used in the experiment. 3-aminophenylboronic acid (0.100 g, 0.7 mmol) and citric acid (0.134 g, 0.7 mmol) were dispersed in 20 mL deionized water. The whole solution was dropped into a 50 mL Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave and the temperature was elevated to 180°C for 8 h. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, a yellow solution was found. The solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for ten minutes to get rid of the unexpected precipitates. The sample was dialyzed against pure water through a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da). Such process was maintained for 24 hours to remove the side products. After the above treatment, the solution was freeze-dried by way of a vacuum freeze dryer for 48 hours. The solid powder-form sample was afforded and kept for measurements in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe(Ⅲ) and ascorbic acid
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation wavelength is the same as the ultra-violet lamp and the value is set at 365 nm. The voltage of photomultiplier is 450 V. The slit width of excitation and emission is fixed at 5.0 nm and 10.0 nm respectively. 1 mg / mL blue emissive CDs stock solution was prepared for all the experimental studies. The solution for 10 μL CDs is to mix different amounts of Fe3+ ions. For various concentrations of ascorbic acid, the 10 μL CDs (1 mg / mL) solution was combined with 90 μL Fe3+ ions. Afterwards, all solution was adjusted to 1mL with distilled water and was stirred at room temperature for 4 min.

To evaluate the selectivity of Fe3+, different ions such as Ag+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Co2+ and Hg2+ (900 μM) were evaluated. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded under the same conditions as Fe3+. The selectivity was measured three times so as to employ the average value.

The generation of nanoscale particles have provided favorable entities for a wide range of applications. Such carbon based nanodomains are different from traditional semiconductor quantum dots and the key point for this species would be their passivated surfaces that caused intense luminescence in the visible or near-infrared region. The carbon dots in this study were easily constructed from two organic molecules and the carbonization in the next step was carried out via hydrothermal treatment for 8 hours (Fig. 1 ).

The utilization of ultra-fine particles in the diameter range less than 10 nm would introduce high surface to volume ratio in the detection mode. The whole process may increase the probability of closer interactions to target species. In addition, the notorious light scattering effect was ruled out in the aqueous solution of quantum dots. The sensing platform was kept to be stable which were very favorable for determination procedures. Hence, the formation of tiny particles and the general microstructure are elucidated in TEM analysis (Fig. 2 and Fig. S1). Here, the adoption of hydrothermal conditions can have a significant impact on the morphology or solubility under high pressure and high temperature. A large amount of small granules with an average diameter of about 2.0 nm were visualized and the particle boundaries were clear and regular.

UV-Vis absorption spectra were given in Fig. 3 . The absorption band at 285 nm was assigned to typical signal from π–π* transitions of unsaturated C=C linkages and these units were corresponding to conjugate structures. The second evidence was originated from the band at longer wavelength at 365 nm. The signal was located in the range of 340-463 nm, which was consistent with the n-π* transition generated by carbonyl or amino groups. These ground state processes were related to the following changes in photoluminescence properties. The quantum yield of the nanodomains was determined to be 11.6 %.

The surface functional groups of carbon dots were analyzed by Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR). As shown in Fig. 4 , the absorption band at 3446 cm−1 was strongly correlated to the stretching vibrations of -NH- or -OH bonds. The narrow and strong band at 1700 cm−1 would be caused by vibration from C=O bonds. The additional absorption peak was derived from asymmetric B−O stretching at 1447 cm−1 [28]. Two broad bands at 1386 cm−1 and 1072 cm−1 were attributed to vibration of C-N bonds and C-B bonds stretching mode [29].

The integration of various functional units onto the surface of carbon dots will allow such nanoplatform capable for detection of different guests. The specific response of carbon dots for Fe3 + has been investigated in several interfering metal ions including Ag+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Hg2+ and Co2+ (Fig. S2). The results revealed that addition of 450 μM other metal ions could hardly interfere with the emission intensity of carbon dots. However, the incorporation of Fe3+ ions can drastically suppress the blue emission. Meanwhile, in the presence of Fe3+ from 5 μM to 450 μM, the fluorescent signal gave rise to a concentration-dependent decrease in intensity and the “on-off” change was observed (Fig. 5). The solution color change could be identified under the irradiation by UV light at 365 nm (inset figure, Fig. 5 ). The correlation between measured data and analytes should be established. As presented in Figure S3, the values of F0- F (F0 refers to the fluorescence intensity without the addition of Fe3+, F is defined as the fluorescence intensity with the addition of Fe3+ at different concentration) versus concentration was conform to a linear function y=0.7598x+ 46.736 and its coefficient square(R2) was 0.9803. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 0.394 μM according to 3SD/slope (SD is the definition of the standard deviation for blank sample, slope is the key component (slope) in the calibration equation). The above analysis verified that quantum dots would be superior to monitor the presence of Fe3+.

The precise control of the emission band would facilitate the application in fluorescent labeling and the recovery of luminescence might become effective in multiple signal transductions [30–34]. The introduction of ascorbic acid could generate the blue emission again and “off-on” effect was observed. As given in Fig. 6 , the concentration dependence in the range of 0- 350 μM ascorbic acid was given. The value of F-F0 (F and F0 are photoluminescence intensity in the absence and presence of AA, respectively) was in agreement with a good linear relation (Fig. S4). Its R2 was 0.9901 and LOD was 0.448 μM. In order to further explore the specificity of carbon dots to ascorbic acid before real application in practical samples, as given in Fig. S5, a variety of potential interfering substances were tried under the same conditions and no significant changes were collected.

In order to clarify the possible process of Fe3+ induced quenching, the zeta potential of carbon dots was determined in the absence and presence of Fe3+.As shown in Fig. 7 , the original quantum dots were negatively charged and the zeta potential value was around -11.09 mV. After the addition of Fe3+, zeta potential value was swift to +1.69 mV, indicating that Fe3+ possesses close contact with the surface groups of carbon dots.

During the performance of dynamic quenching, the excited state molecules were returned to the ground state by collision with the nearby molecules, and their excitation energy would be largely released by way of energy transfer or charge transfer. Static quenching is normally referred to the assembly of non-luminescent ground state complexes between optical-active species and quenching agents, or the generation of effective quenching modes by excited fluorescent molecules [34,35]. Therefore, UV spectrum can be clearly differentiate the dynamic quenching from static quenching [36]. As provided in Fig. 3, the absorption curve of CDs-Fe3 + was completely overlapped with the signal of pure carbon dots, and there was no new UV absorption peak, so we tentatively say that quenching mode was essentially driven by dynamic way via non-radiative charge transfer between the quencher and the excited state of the nanomaterial due to electrostatic attraction between carbon dots and Fe3+.

We provided a simple one-step method based on hydrothermal treatment to assemble carbon quantum dots (CDs) with blue emission. As versatile luminescent materials, the rapid development for the assembly of carbon dots with mono-guest sensing purpose has been observed, but the systematic work concerning double targets determination and the evaluation of reversible capability would be rather limited. The main achievement in this study would be the realization of of novel nano-particles for diverse functions. Here, the emission quenching of carbon dots by Fe3+ was obtained and the reversible way was proved in the presence of ascorbic acid. These facts would contribute to the synthesis of an “on-off-on” sequential optical sensor and might shed new lights on the analysis of specific interactions between charged nanoparticles and guest species.
Yaowen Liu: Writing – original draft. Junjie Chen: Data curation. Qianming Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that no conflicts of interest concerning the submission.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Q. M. thanks grants from Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2021A1515010324).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2022.102112.
